select_when: A plan faces deep uncertainty where average-case forecasts may badly understate tail exposure or fragility.
The team needs to shift from prediction theater toward vulnerability reduction, scenario analysis, or stress-testing because unknown unknowns matter.
A complex system could fail through cascading effects even if each individual component looks tolerable on its own.
The next move should improve resilience and decision quality under surprise rather than pretending the rare event can be forecast precisely.
avoid_when: Black Swan language is being used as vague fear rhetoric even though concrete resilience or prioritization moves are available.
An aberration is being treated as the new normal in a way that would distort strategy and create unnecessary paralysis.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the model centered on unknown unknowns, fragility in complex systems, and preparation discipline rather than prediction theater.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broader uncertainty sprawl because the raw source is strongest on resilience, systems thinking, scenario analysis, and anti-hindsight discipline.
open_questions: The output type compresses a source that also spans decision-quality versus outcome-quality and execution checklists.

select_when.note: The raw source is explicit on unknown unknowns, vulnerability reduction, deep uncertainty, and the fragility of complex disjunctive systems.

avoid_when.note: Both anti-activation conditions are grounded in source warnings against tail-risk theater and treating aberrations as the strategic norm.

input_type.note: The source clearly activates on tail-risk and fragility problems, while the typed input remains a normalized routing label.

output_type.note: The source supports vulnerability reduction and stress-testing moves; the output label compresses that into an operational artifact.

reasoning_types.note: Black Swan reasoning is strongest as probabilistic systems thinking about fragility and preparation rather than direct prediction.
